name: Linares and Giribaile Reservoir Loop
description: Cycle the Linares – Greenway Guadalimar, a 21.7-mile route through olive groves, river scenery, and railway history in Jaén, Spain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Linares traverse a diverse landscape in the province of Jaén, Andalusia. The region is characterized by extensive olive groves, often referred to as the "sea of olives," and features terrain ranging from rolling hills to the foothills of the Sierra Morena. Cyclists can explore dedicated Vías Verdes, which are repurposed railway lines, and follow routes along river valleys. This varied topography provides a range of experiences for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Linares The most popular road cycling route is Linares, Bailén and Baños de, a 33.9 miles (54.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 33 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the expansive olive groves and rolling countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Linares and Giribaile Reservoir Loop, a moderate 21.7 miles (35.0 km) path. This route features varied terrain and leads to the scenic Giribaile Reservoir. Local road cyclists also love the Castillo De Canena – Church of the Holy Cross loop from Antigua Estación de Almería-San José, a 37.7 miles (60.7 km) trail leading through historical areas and more challenging climbs, often completed in about 3 hours 7 minutes. Road cycling around Linares is defined by vast olive groves, rolling hills, and routes along river valleys and repurposed Vías Verdes. The network offers options for various ability levels, from gentle rides to more challenging ascents into the Sierra Morena foothills. The routes in Linares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 7 reviews. More than 1,100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Linares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Linares?
A: Road cycling around Linares offers a diverse landscape. You'll primarily encounter the vast "sea of olives" with rolling hills, but routes also extend into the foothills of the Sierra Morena, providing more challenging ascents. Additionally, there are flatter sections along river valleys and dedicated Vías Verdes (Greenways) that follow repurposed railway lines, offering smooth, traffic-free surfaces.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Linares?
A: Yes, Linares offers routes suitable for various skill levels. While many routes are moderate or difficult, there are options for beginners. The Vías Verdes, such as the Vía Verde del Guadalimar or sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite, often feature gentle slopes and traffic-free environments, making them ideal for less experienced cyclists or those looking for a relaxed ride.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Linares?
A: There are over 50 road cycling routes available around Linares on komoot, offering a wide range of distances and difficulties to explore the region's unique landscapes.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see while road cycling near Linares?
A: While cycling near Linares, you'll be immersed in the iconic "sea of olives." Routes often pass by historical landmarks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3144645" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Burgalimar Castle&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter natural monuments such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5323958" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;views of the Rumblar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, or the scenic Guadalimar River. The region is also a gateway to the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, known for its mountains and waterfalls.

Q: Can I cycle along any Vías Verdes (Greenways) in the Linares area?
A: Absolutely. Linares is a great starting point for exploring several Vías Verdes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123756" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de Linares&lt;/a&gt; offers a winding descent towards the Guadalimar River, and the Vía Verde del Guadalimar begins near the Linares-Baeza train station, providing picturesque views of olive groves and distant Sierra Mágina. Sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite are also accessible, known for their gentle slopes and charming old railway stations converted into cafes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Linares?
A: The best time for road cycling in Linares is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is generally mild and pleasant, making for comfortable riding conditions. Summers can be very hot, and winters, while generally mild, can bring cooler temperatures.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Linares?
A: Yes, there are several excellent loop routes available. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996241984/linares-and-giribaile-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linares and Giribaile Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 35 km path featuring varied terrain and leading to the scenic Giribaile Reservoir. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31442841" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo De Canena – Church of the Holy Cross loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through historical areas with more challenging climbs.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Linares?
A: The road cycling routes in Linares are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the unique experience of cycling through the extensive olive groves, the varied terrain that caters to different skill levels, and the opportunity to discover historical landmarks and natural beauty along the way.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Linares?
A: Experienced riders will find plenty of challenging options around Linares, particularly those venturing into the foothills of the Sierra Morena. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31442841" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo De Canena – Church of the Holy Cross loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 60 km with significant elevation gain, or the extensive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123697" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument Navas De Tolosa – Vadollano Roman Bridge loop&lt;/a&gt;, offer demanding climbs and rewarding descents.

Q: Can I find routes that combine cycling with views of reservoirs or water bodies?
A: Yes, you can. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996241984/linares-and-giribaile-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linares and Giribaile Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a great option, leading directly to the scenic Giribaile Reservoir. Additionally, the Rumblar Reservoir Dam, located within the Sierra Morena region, is a significant ecological site, and routes in its vicinity offer views of this important water body.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through historical sites or offer cultural insights?
A: Many routes around Linares offer glimpses into the region's rich history. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123756" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de Toros de Linares – Vadollano Roman Bridge loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the historic Roman bridge. Other routes may lead you past ancient olive mills or through charming white villages, especially along sections of the Vía Verde del Aceite, where old railway stations have been repurposed into cultural stops.
tips: The Plaza del Ayuntamiento is the administrative and cultural center of the town. Linares is the second most important municipality in the province of Jaén. The plaza area also includes the Casa de la Ammunition and the Rafael Museum.

The Linares Bullring, also known as the Santa Margarita Bullring, was built in 1866, and inaugurated a year later with a Miura bullfight, with the presence of the swordsmen Antonio Carmona, "El Gordito" and Manuel Carmona , "Baker".

   The most well-known and outstanding event that occurred in this arena is the death of the bullfighter Manolete in August 1947, gored by the bull Islero. Next to the gang gate there is a ceramic and marble plaque commemorating that tragic afternoon at the fair.

   It has a ring 50 meters in diameter and its alley 1.30 meters wide. It has a capacity of 10,500 spectators.
Ruin of a stone bridge over the Guarrizas river from Roman times. Currently, a modern bridge crosses the A-312 road, providing a good viewpoint of the old bridge.

faqs.question: Is the Linares – Greenway Guadalimar suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally considered easy, featuring a largely flat profile with minimal steep slopes. It's an excellent option for beginners and families, though its 'moderate' difficulty rating primarily reflects its distance rather than challenging terrain.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Greenway Guadalimar?
faqs.answer: The trail features a 'double firm' surface, consisting of compacted gravel and asphalt treatment. While generally smooth, be aware that some sections, particularly between kilometers 3.5 and 4, can become muddy during periods of heavy rainfall.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Linares – Greenway Guadalimar?
faqs.answer: The trail officially begins near the Linares-Baeza train station. You can typically find parking facilities in the vicinity of the station to access the trailhead.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse scenery, including lush riverside greenery along the Guadalimar River and expansive olive groves. You'll also encounter remnants of its railway past, such as viaducts and tunnels. Notable highlights include the Town Hall Square of Linares, Plaza de Toros de Linares, and the Vadollano Roman Bridge.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this greenway?
faqs.answer: To avoid muddy sections and enjoy the most pleasant weather, the drier seasons are generally best. Spring and autumn offer comfortable temperatures for cycling, while summer can be quite hot in this region of Spain.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Linares – Greenway Guadalimar?
faqs.answer: Greenways in Spain are generally dog-friendly, provided your pet is kept on a leash and you clean up after them. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and doesn't disturb other users or local wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to use the trail?
faqs.answer: No, as a repurposed railway line, the Vía Verde del Guadalimar (Greenway Guadalimar) is typically free to access and does not require any permits or entrance fees.

faqs.question: Are there rest areas or amenities along the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is well-equipped with six designated rest areas. These areas typically feature benches, picnic tables, litter bins, and bike racks for your convenience. You'll also find signage, information panels, and kilometer markers.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Vía Verde de Linares, Camino Natural Via Verde del Guadalimar, Camino del Puente Mocho, and Vía Verde del Guadalimar.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a transition from riverside floodplains to vast olive groves, a hallmark of the Jaén province. As you head north, the trail ascends slightly, offering magnificent panoramic views of the Sierra Mágina mountains in the distance.

faqs.question: Is the Linares – Greenway Guadalimar a loop trail?
faqs.answer: No, the Linares – Greenway Guadalimar is primarily an out-and-back route, following the former railway line. You will return along the same path to your starting point.
